coredata.dc:title: Improved ultraviolet photodetector performances using solution-processed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/ZnO hybrid thin films
coredata.dc:creator: Thyda, Lavanya
Naresh, Koppula
Joseph, Joel K
Suneetha, S.
Jeyanthi, C Esther
Amaladass, P.
Selvaraju, C.
Thangaraju, Kuppusamy
coredata.dc:description: We fabricated the solution-processed photodetectors using the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/zinc oxide (NCQDs/ZnO) hybrid films and characterized using ultraviolet (UV) spot-light of 365 nm at 1 mW/cm2. The NCQDs adsorbed onto the surface and/or grain boundaries of ZnO crystallites control ZnO particle size (by capping) in the NCQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films, resulting in the improved film quality and decreased the dark current in the devices. The NCQDs to ZnO [%(w/v)] ratio of 1.2 × 10−3 in the hybrid film UV photodetector exhibits the stable and reproducible photoresponses with the highest responsivity & external quantum efficiency (which are ∼3 times higher), and detectivity (∼7.5 times higher) than the pristine ZnO based photodetector, attributed to the effective transfer of photogenerated electrons from NCQDs to ZnO nanoparticles in the hybrid film. The solution-processed NCQDs/ZnO hybrid films will be very much useful to improve the UV photodetector performances for the cost-effective commercial applications.

head.ce:title: Improved ultraviolet photodetector performances using solution-processed nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/ZnO hybrid thin films

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We fabricated the solution-processed photodetectors using the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/zinc oxide (NCQDs/ZnO) hybrid films and characterized using ultraviolet (UV) spot-light of 365 nm at 1 mW/cm2. The NCQDs adsorbed onto the surface and/or grain boundaries of ZnO crystallites control ZnO particle size (by capping) in the NCQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films, resulting in the improved film quality and decreased the dark current in the devices. The NCQDs to ZnO [%(w/v)] ratio of 1.2 × 10−3 in the hybrid film UV photodetector exhibits the stable and reproducible photoresponses with the highest responsivity & external quantum efficiency (which are ∼3 times higher), and detectivity (∼7.5 times higher) than the pristine ZnO based photodetector, attributed to the effective transfer of photogenerated electrons from NCQDs to ZnO nanoparticles in the hybrid film. The solution-processed NCQDs/ZnO hybrid films will be very much useful to improve the UV photodetector performances for the cost-effective commercial applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors are very important and widely used in various applications such as environmental monitoring, ozone layer monitoring, water sterilization, optical communications, civilian and military, warning systems in missile plume detection, forest-fire detection [1–4]. It is highly necessary to protect against increasing UV radiation on earth's surface because high UV intensity is harmful for humans, biological systems, and environment. The conventional silicon-photodiodes for UV photodetection experience severe limitations such as poor selectivity at visible and infrared (IR) wavelength range, required ultrahigh vacuum condition and high voltage, shallow penetration depth (less than 20 nm) of UV light (∼370 nm) in silicon, high surface recombination of UV generated photocarriers due to ultrashort absorption lengths, need to be cooled down to reduce the dark current, and degradation of devices upon continuous UV exposure [1,3,5]. Although various semiconducting materials (such as SiC, GaN, TiO2, SnO2, etc.) have emerged as efficient candidates for UV light detection, Zinc oxide (ZnO) became as the prime candidate for various optoelectronic devices and UV photodetectors due to its excellent properties such as wide band gap energy (∼3.37 eV) falling in the UV wavelength region, large exciton binding energy (∼60 meV), UV light sensitivity, ability of operating at high temperatures and in harsh environments, non-toxic and abundance on earth crest, and ease of highly transparent (in the visible range) and high quality thin film formation by cost-effective methods (such as solution processed techniques) [6–10]. The Schottky UV photodetector based on the epitaxial ZnO thin film grown on sapphire substrate exhibited the fast photoresponse time in the order of microseconds (µs), ascribed to that the growth of highly crystalline ZnO thin films with suitable metal contacts is essential for the improved device performances [11]. The high quality ZnO thin films deposited by various sophisticated techniques such as metal-organic chemical vapor deposition, pulsed laser deposition, and sputtering methods have been extensively studied for the UV photodetection applications over the conventional devices, showing that the UV photoconduction and charge transport depend on the stoichiometry (oxygen vacancies), trap densities, oxygen gas (O2) adsorption and desorption on the surface of ZnO thin films [12–14]. The UV photodetectors based on ZnO nanoparticles have also been demonstrated with high on/off ratio (the ratio of photocurrent to dark current; 106), low dark current, and high responsivity (61 A/W under the UV illumination of 1.06 mW/cm2 (370 nm)) [1,15]. The closely packed ZnO nanowire array grown on n-type Si (100) substrate by magnetron co-sputtering method (ZnO nanowire array/n-type Si heterojunction) revealed good diode characteristics with high rectification ratio of ∼1600 at 4 V, demonstrated as it is very useful for the UV light detection [16]. Das et al. has fabricated efficient UV photodetectors based on the one-dimensional ZnO single nanowire (NW) grown by electron-beam lithographic technique and reported that the barrier height modulation and interface states at the schottky junction play a major role for the improved photoresponse under the UV illumination in addition to the adsorption and desorption of oxygen gas atoms on the surface of ZnO single NW [17]. The fabricated UV photodetector device using highly crystalline interconnected ZnO nanotetrapods grown by burner-flame transport synthesis technique and bridged between pre-patterned gold electrodes (in-situ integration) exhibited the high on/off ratio (∼4.5 × 103 at 2.4 V) and fast rise/decay times (68 ms/32 ms) under the UV light illumination (365 nm; ∼15 mW/cm2) due to the energy barrier alteration at nanojunction formed in the nano-tetrapod networks for the efficient photoconduction [5]. The highly crystalline n-type ZnO nanorods vertically grown on p-type GaN substrate forming p-GaN/n-ZnO nanorod heterojunction showed good rectification ratio (5.4 at bias voltage of 3 V) and fast UV (He-Cd laser light of 325 nm) photoresponse [18]. The p-i-n diode structure has also been made to improve the performances of p-GaN/n-ZnO heterojunction-based UV photodetector by inserting the insulating layer of CsPbBr3 (p-GaN/CsPbBr3/n-ZnO heterojunction), exhibiting highly improved responsivity (44.53 mA/W) and detectivity (2.03 × 1012 Jones) under zero bias voltage [19]. The ZnO nanostructured heterojunctions play a very important role to improve the performance of UV light detection as reported elsewhere [20–23].

In the recent years as an alternative and outperforming counterpart to inorganic semiconductor nanostructured materials, a clause of eco-friendly zero dimensional carbonaceous materials having sizes around 10 nm and below, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (quasi-spherical shaped nanoparticles without apparent aggregation) consisting of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon core structure with diverse functional groups (such as carboxyl and hydroxyl) on the surface, has emerged as a rising star in the carbon family in the field of chemistry, physics, material science, and interdisciplinary research [24–27]. The CQDs and heteroatom (such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and phosphorus) doped CQDs have widely been utilized in various device applications such as electroluminescent devices, photovoltaic cells, deep UV photodetectors, phototransistors, bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery. It possesses the excellent properties of nontoxicity, abundant sources, high quantum yield, good water solubility for solution-processability, chemical stability, availability of suitable chemical reactive sites, resistance to photo-bleaching, high absorption coefficient, tunable electronic and optical band gap by quantum confinement effect, tunable photoluminescence (PL), large separation in absorption and emission peaks, extremely fast electron transfer rate, high mechanical strength, high carrier life time, and high charge carrier mobility in the devices [28–37]. Ghosh et al. has reported a stable and highly efficient visible-blind UV photodetector [with a remarkable responsivity of 66,200 A/W and detectivity of 1.78 × 1015 Jones under the incident UV light illumination (365 nm; 10 µW/cm²) at low bias of 2 V] based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/ZnO nanorods heterojunction by immobilizing GQDs onto the vertically grown/aligned ZnO nanorods, attributed to the lower density of trap states and low potential barrier at the GQDs/ZnO nanorod interface for providing the excellent electron transport and charge transfer to conduction band of ZnO [2]. The rapid thermal process-synthesized GQDs wrapped the vertically grown ZnO nanorods array (ZnO/GQDs core/shell heterostructures) demonstrated excellent UV photosensing properties (with a responsivity of 45.71 A/W) due to the higher photoabsorption, photocarrier generation, and enhanced carrier mobility with reduced recombination of GQDs [38]. A hybrid photoactive layer consisting of CQDs decorated onto the ZnO nanorods (NRs) in the UV photodetector exhibited the higher detectivity (8.33 × 1012 Jones) because of CQDs acting as charge control units which enable photogenerated electron-hole separation and induce the effective charge extraction from CQDs and ZnO NRs [39]. Guo et al. has demonstrated that the spin-coated hybrid thin films of ZnO quantum dots/carbon nanodots exhibit the improved UV detection performances (responsivity of 1.7 × 106 A/W and the detectivity of 3.1 × 1017 cmHz1/2/W) under the weak UV light illumination (12 nW/cm2), attributed to the increased charge carrier (photogenerated electron-hole pairs) separation at ZnO/Carbon dots interface [40]. In the recent report, solution processed in-situ self-assembled CQDs layers/Si heterostructure based self-powered photodetectors manifest enhanced device performances (9.4 mA/W and 5.9 × 1012 Jones with rise/decay time of <14/25 µs) due to efficient charge separation and transport from CQDs to Ag electrode [41]. The CQDs effectively sensitize titanium dioxide (TiO2) NRs in self-powered UV photodetectors consisting of n-type TiO2
CQDs/p-type poly(2,2′:5′,2′-trithiophene) (PTTh) heterojunction and achieves the maximum device performances (1.45 mA/W and 4.5 × 1010 Jones with rise/decay time of ∼91/3 ms) [42]. Hsiao et al. has also reported the improved photodetection performances using dual-size graded CQDs (bottom layer with a CQDs size of ∼10 nm acting as main light harvester and top layer with CQDs size of 6 nm featuring as electron blocking layer as well as hole extractor)/Si heterostructure which reduces the dark current and in turn enhances the device performances [43]. The key parameters of photodetectors based on CQDs and related heterostructure published in the various research reports are given in Table 1 for the comparison purpose. In our recent report, we have prepared the highly transparent (above 81% of average transmittance) nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs; size of ∼2.5 to 3 nm) incorporated ZnO hybrid films by spin-coating method [24]. It has been reported that the incorporated NCQDs got adsorbed (chemisorbed) onto the surface of the formed ZnO nanoparticles and non-radiatively transfer the energy of excited UV light (325 nm) to ZnO, giving rise to near-band-edge emission (380 nm) [24].

In this work, we characterize the synthesized NCQDs using time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopic study which ascertains the presence of electrically active surface functional groups for electrical conductivity and/or charge transfer of NCQDs. The NCQDs absorb the UV energy (365 nm) and transfer it non-radiatively to ZnO nanoparticles (giving rise to its defect related visible emission) in the sol-gel derived spin-coated NCQDs incorporated ZnO (NCQDs/ZnO) hybrid thin films. The fabricated solution-processed UV photodetectors based on the prepared spin-coated highly transparent NCQDs/ZnO hybrid films exhibit the highest responsivity & external quantum efficiency (which are ∼3 times higher), and detectivity (∼7.5 times higher) than that of pristine ZnO based UV photodetector device.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs): The widely-used hydrothermal synthesis method for preparation of NCQDs powder was adapted as-such from the earlier reports published elsewhere [24,44,45]. A transparent solution was obtained by dissolving the citric acid (C6H8O7: purity of 99%; Merck Pvt Ltd, India) and urea (CH4N2O: purity of 99%; Merck Pvt Ltd, India) as sources of organic carbon and nitrogen, respectively, in double-ionized (DI) water (100 mL). The dissolved solution having the molar ratio of 1:3 (mM) of citric acid and urea taken in an autoclave (Teflon-lined stainless-steel) of volume of 150 mL was heated in the vacuum oven at 190 °C for 8 h followed by natural cooling to room temperature. The obtained reacted brown-black aqueous solution containing NCQDs was filtered out using membrane syringe filter (pore size: 0.22 µm) and the final hydrothermally synthesized black-colored NCQDs powder was vacuum dried (to evaporate the undesired residue) at 130 °C for 12 h followed by thorough cleaning (rinsing in ethanol solvent and vacuum dried at 110 °C for 2 h) continued twice.

NCQDs/ZnO hybrid precursor: The ZnO sol-gel precursor was prepared by dissolving zinc acetate-dihydrate [Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O; Sigma Aldrich (purity of 98.5%), India] of 1.646 g (0.5 M) in 2‑methoxy-ethanol (C3H8O2: purity of 99%; SRL, India) solvent (15 mL) for a weight to volume percentage [%(w/v)] of zinc acetate to be 10.97. A monoethanolamine solvent (MEA: 99%; SRL, India) used as a stabilizer (0.453 mL) was drop-wise added in order to have MEA and Zinc acetate in 1:1 molar ratio [24]. The precursor (sol-gel) (solution-1) was obtained after allowing it for gelation for 24 h and filtered for the spin-coating of pristine film (film a). The synthesized NCQDs powder samples with the weight of 0.4, 0.8, and 1.2 mg were dispersed separately in three 2-methoxyethanol solvent containers (3 mL + 3 µL of MEA stabilizer) to obtain solution-2 [NCQDs with 13.33 × 10−3%(w/v)], solution-3 [26.66 × 10−3%(w/v)], and solution-4 [40 × 10−3%(w/v)], respectively. Each NCQDs dispersed solutions were kept for ultrasonication (30 min) and stirred for 16 hrs at 65 °C in order to get the higher dispersion without any apparent aggregation. The three (each 3 mL) hybrid (NCQDs/ZnO) sol-gel precursors were prepared for film b {2 mL of solution-1 (ZnO sol-gel precursor) [0.33 M or 7.32%(w/v) of Zinc acetate]+ 1 mL of solution-2 of NCQDs [4.4 × 10−3%(w/v)]}, film c {2 mL of solution-1 + 1 mL of solution-3 of NCQDs [8.8 × 10−3%(w/v)]}, and film d {2 mL of solution-1 + 1 mL of solution-4 of NCQDs [11.1 × 10−3%(w/v)]}. The nominal weight-to-volume percentage [%(w/v)] ratio of NCQDs to ZnO in the hybrid precursors is 0.6 × 10−3(film b), 1.2 × 10−3(film c), and 1.8 × 10−3(film d) as given in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of N-CQDS/ZnO hybrid films and instrumentations
ce:section.ce:para: The above prepared ZnO sol-gel precursor (solution-1 for the film a) and the highly dispersed hybrid sol-gel precursors (for the films b - d) were utilized to deposit the highly uniform films (a - d) onto the glass substrates using the Spin-coating unit (Apex Instruments; India; Ezspin-A1 model). The substrates (thickness of ∼1.3 mm, Suryam Microslides, India) were essentially cleaned following the step-by-step cleaning procedure reported elsewhere [24,28,46]. The thoroughly cleaned glass substrates were further ultra-sonicated (10 min) in acetone and continued the sonication process (each 10 min) using isopropyl alcohol (isopropanol) and DI water, respectively. The vacuum drying was carried out at each step for 10 min at 120 °C. The plasma cleaning at the high-power density was conducted using Harrick Plasma cleaner (Harrick Plasma, USA; PDC-002 model) in order to remove out the oxide contamination formed onto the substrates. The process of sol-gel derived spin-coating was carried out at ∼2000 rpm for 30 s followed by drying at 180 °C for 10 min on a hot plate under the normal ambient and the same spin coating process was repeated for 5 times which yielded the optimal film thickness of around 200 nm for each thin film (a - d). The deposited films (a - d) were kept in a tubular furnace under the normal ambient for post annealing at 350 °C for 2 hrs which improved the film quality significantly. The UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Agilent Carry 5000) was used to measure the UV–visible transmission spectra of the deposited thin films (a - d). The PL spectra were recorded using the Fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin-Yvon, Fluorolog-3 model). The PL decays of hydrothermally prepared NCQDs powder (ultrasonicated for 5 min in DI water and then filled in quartz cuvette) were recorded using time-correlated single photon counting method (TCSPC, Hamamatsu, Japan) at an excitation laser wavelength of 375 nm for the range of observed emissions (434, 442, 450, 477 nm) and analyzed by using the software provided by the IBM (DAS-6 analysis software) [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of UV photodetectors and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The solution-processed UV photodetectors were fabricated based on the above-described film a for Device A as reference device and the hybrid films (b for Device B, c for Device C, and d for Device D). The highly transparent and uniform films (a - d) of thickness ∼200 nm were coated on to the cleaned glass substrates and post-annealed as described above. The prepared thin films (a - d) were loaded into the thermal evaporation unit (VR Technologies, Bangalore, India) for the deposition of aluminum (Al) electrodes (thickness of ∼120 nm) using the metal masks under the vacuum of ∼5 mPa to obtain the UV photodetectors (A - D), respectively. The device channel with active area of 8 mm2 [length (4 mm) x width (2 mm)] was built between two Al electrodes deposited in the middle of the substrate for each device (A - D). The electrical characteristics and photoresponses of the devices (A - D) were assessed under the UV light illumination (UV spot light source: 365 nm, Hamamatsu, Japan, L9588–06A model) having the shutter auto-off and spot diameter of 1 cm with the UV power of 1 mW/cm2 focused on the surface of the active channel by using a Keithley's current/voltage source/measure unit (SMU) (2450 model) under the normal ambient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical studies of NCQDs and N-CQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of hydrothermally synthesized NCQDs have been discussed in detail in our previous report published elsewhere [24]. Briefly, to use it in the fabrication of efficient UV photodetectors, NCQDs powder exhibited a strong broad characteristic absorption band (peak at ∼333 nm) along with tail absorption covering the visible range, showing the optical band gap of 2.72 eV [24]. The characteristic PL emission has been observed at around 434 nm (peak wavelength: λem) upon the excitation wavelength (λex) of 320 nm and it has also been confirmed the presence of active surface functional groups from the excitation dependent PL (red-shifted) studies [24,48–50].

Fig. 1 shows the PL decay profiles of the NCQDs solution collected at a pulsed excitation laser wavelength of 375 nm for different emission wavelengths (434, 442, 450, and 477 nm) using time-correlated single photon counting technique. All the PL decays were best-fitted with a tri-exponential function which results in three decay components. The lifetimes (τ1, τ2, τ3), their relative amplitudes, and the best-fit factor (χ2) of the tri-exponential decay analysis are given in the Table 2. At λex = 375 nm (pulsed UV laser excitation), the decay fit of the characteristic PL emission (λem: 434 nm) of NCQDs results in (i) two short lifetimes [τ1 = 0.49 ns & τ2 = 3.43 ns] attributed to the fast radiative emission from the graphitic core structure (sp2-hybridized carbon), and (ii) a long lifetime (τ3 = 10.29 ns) due to delayed emission via internal crossing from the different O and N containing active/radiative functional groups present on the surface and/or at edges of NCQDs [25,51]. It is also observed that at the same laser excitation wavelength of 375 nm, the PL decay components slightly increase for increasing PL emission wavelengths from 434 to 477 nm (inset of Fig. 1) which corroborate the radiative decay of excitons from the sub-band energy levels below the LUMO level of NCQDs, showing the presence of electrically active/radiative surface functional groups responsible for the electrical conductivity of NCQDs and/or the transfer of excited charge carriers from NCQDs in their hybrid composites.

Fig. 2 shows the transmittance spectra of the spin-coated pristine ZnO (film a) and hybrid (NCQDs/ZnO) thin films (b - d). The pristine ZnO thin film (film a) exhibits the high transmittance (∼98% at the 550 nm) in the visible region and slightly decreased without any significant change in the transmittance (∼96% for the film b, ∼95% for the film c, and ∼96% for the film d at 550 nm) upon incorporation of NCQDs content in the hybrid films, ascertaining that the NCQDs act as light scattering centers [9,24,46,52]. The observed sharp absorption band-edge of the pristine film a in the UV region is slightly increased to higher energy (blue-shifted) in the films (a→d) upon slight increase of NCQDs to ZnO ratio [%(w/v)], corroborating the shifting of Fermi energy level into the conduction band due to the decreasing size of ZnO crystallites [24,53]. The slightly increased optical band-gap upon increasing NCQDs content in the hybrid films (as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 and given in Table 2) is related to the quantum confinement effect [24,52–54].

The normalized PL spectrum [Fig. 3 (i)] of the film a at characteristic excitation of λex = 325 nm exhibits the intense finger-print near-band-edge excitonic UV emission (∼384 nm) along with a broad weak visible emission (peaking at ∼532 nm) which is most often related to the different types of structural defects formed in the solution processed ZnO thin films [9,24]. The intensity ratio of defect-related emission peak (I532) with respect to the band-edge emission (I384) for the pristine film (a) is found to be 0.31. The film b with 0.6 × 10−3{nominal ratio of NCQDs to ZnO [%(w/v)]} at the same excitation of 325 nm revealed the increased band-edge emission at ∼384 nm with decreased defect-related visible emission as compared to pristine film (a) [24]. It is also found that the emission intensity ratio (I532/I384) (0.11 for film c and 0.21 for film d) is significantly increased for increasing content of NCQDs in the hybrid films (b - d) in comparison with that (0.05) of film b, attributed to the fact that the NCQDs absorb the excited UV energy and essentially transfer it non-radiatively to defect energy levels of ZnO as well [24].

In order to understand the possible transfer of UV energy absorbed by NCQDs to the defect-energy levels of ZnO in the solution processed hybrid films, the films (a - d) were subjected to the excitation of λex = 365 nm (UV light) [The same wavelength (365 nm) of UV spot-light source used for UV photodetectors characterization in this study] to obtain their PL spectra as shown in the Fig. 3(ii). The widely observed weak defect related broad emission of ZnO in the visible region is obtained for the film a, attributed to the presence of various defect energy levels below the conduction band of ZnO in the solution processed film [52–55]. The film (b) showed the intense PL emission peak at ∼439 nm which is attributed to the characteristic emission of NCQDs, along with a long-tail emission engulfing the visible emission of ZnO corresponding to the defects, ascertaining that the adsorption of NCQDs predominantly onto the ZnO's surface as reported in the earlier studies and give rise to its characteristic visible emission [1,9,24,28]. The slightly increased amount of NCQDs in the film c suppressed the emission intensity of characteristic NCQDs emission (∼439 nm), ascribing the adsorption of NCQDs on the grain boundaries of ZnO crystallites via chemical interactions (chemisorptions) and control the size of ZnO particles in the nano-scale by capping the particles in the bulk of the hybrid film. For further increasing amount (NCQDs) in film d {with the ratio of 1.8 × 10−3}, it exhibited the significantly increased intensity of defect-related visible emission of ZnO along with the considerable contribution from the characteristic NCQDs emission, supporting that under the excitation of λex = 365 nm, the adsorbed NCQDs in the hybrid thin film (d) not only emit at the characteristic PL emission range (∼439 nm) but also transfer the energy to the energy levels of ZnO nanoparticles to give rise to defect-related visible emission. These results corroborate that the NCQDs get adsorbed onto the ZnO nanoparticles in the NCQDs/ZnO films and transfer the absorbed energy non-radiatively to ZnO nanoparticles under the excitation of λex = 365 nm, which will be useful to improve the performance of the hybrid thin film-based UV (365 nm) photodetectors.

In order to realize the performance of above films, the solution-processed UV photodetectors were fabricated using film a (Device A as a reference device) and hybrid films (film b for Device B, film c for Device C, and film d for Device D). The fabricated devices (A - D) are having active area of around 8 mm2 [length (4 mm) x width (2 mm)] between two Al electrodes deposited onto the thin films as the schematic representation shown in the Fig. 4 (i) along with the energy level diagrams [Fig. 4(ii)] of the materials used in the devices. Fig. 5 shows the current density - voltage (J-V) characteristics of the fabricated UV photodetectors (Devices A - D) under the dark and UV light illumination (365 nm) at the optical (UV) power density of 1 mW/cm2. The linear J-V characteristics of devices ascribe the ohmic nature of the Al contacts. Device A exhibited the linear increase in the dark current (ohmic behavior) on increasing applied bias voltage [36/74 (+8/−8 V), 16.4/19 (+3/−3 V), and 7.2/7.5 nA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)]. Under the UV light illumination (365 nm at 1 mW/cm2), the photo-generated charge carriers constitute the higher light current density (ohmic behavior) [35/33 (+8/−8 V), 12/11 (+3/−3 V), and 4.5/4.3 µA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] along with high UV light-current (IUV-ON) to dark current ratio (IUV-ON/IDark ratio) of 977/445 (+8/−8 V), 730/586 (+3/−3 V), and 625/634 (+1/−1 V) (Fig. 5). Fig. 6 shows the photoresponse curves of the devices (A - D) under the switching (ON/OFF for every 30 s) of UV light illumination (365 nm at 1 mW/cm2) at the different fixed bias voltages (±1 V and ±3 V). Device A based on pristine ZnO thin film exhibited the consistent switching currents even after three cycles at every fixed bias voltage, showing the good device stability and reproducibility. The rise time (τr) of the photocurrent (when UV illumination is ON) is defined as the time required for the photocurrent to rise from 10% to 90% of the peak value and the decay time (τd) (when UV illumination is OFF) is the required time to fall from 90% to 10% of the photocurrent maximum value, showing the τr (10 s) and τd (14.7 s) for the device A at a fixed biasing of 1 V and τr (9.5 s) and τd (14.5 s) at −1 V. It has widely been reported that the oxygen (gas) molecules from the ambient get adsorbed onto the surface of n-type ZnO by capturing its free electrons, becoming oxygen ions (anions) [O2 (gas) + eˉ → O2ˉ (adsorbed anion)] and leaving the large potential gradient (causing the wide depletion region) near the surface which is resulting in the lower dark current [1,12,44,56–58]. The electron-hole pairs are generated in the ZnO when the UV illumination (365 nm at 1 mW/cm2) is ON and the photo-generated holes diffuse to the surface of ZnO along the potential gradient to desorb the oxygen molecules from the surface [h + + O2ˉ → O2 (gas) (desorbed)] and/or get trapped near the surface, decreasing the depletion width near the surface. Thus, the photogenerated free electrons constitute the increased photocurrent in the device A. On subsequent switching of UV light illumination to OFF, the holes trapped at the surface states of ZnO constitute the persistent photocurrent, resulting in increased decay time and/or the consequent readsorpition of oxygen molecules on the surface of ZnO increases the width of depletion region near the surface, reducing the device current to the level of dark current [1,12,44,56–59]. The photocurrent response is significantly increased and stable when the bias voltage is increased to 3 V at the same UV light power (1 mW/cm2), attributed to the fact that the spin-coated ZnO thin film is highly crystalline and having large photoexcited free electrons (with the substantial prevention of recombination of photogenerated charge carries) to drive them to the respective electrodes under the increased applied electric field [9,17,60]. The rise and decay times of the devices (A - D) at the biasing voltages of ±1 or ±3 V under the UV (365 nm) illumination of 1 mW/cm2 are summarized in Table 3 .

The fabricated UV photodetector (Device B) using the hybrid thin film (b) showed the significantly decreased dark current density with ohmic behavior [6.1/7.6 (+8/−8 V), 2.4/1.7 (+3/−3 V), and 1.4/1 nA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] when compared to the reference device A based on pristine ZnO film (a), attributed to the adsorbed (chemisorbed) NCQDs onto the surface and/or ZnO's grain boundaries and also increase the width of the low conductivity depletion region (potential gradient) near the surface/grain boundaries resulting in the reduced dark current [1,12,24,44,57,58]. The device B resulted in the reduced light current (Under the UV illumination of 365 nm at 1 mW/cm2) [17/16 (+8/−8 V), 4.6/5.1 (+3/−3 V), and 1/2.1 µA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] when compared to the device A, corroborating the incorporated NCQDs which get adsorbed at surface and/or ZnO's grain boundaries and absorb the UV energy which in turn results in the slight reduction of the photogenerated charge carriers in ZnO [24]. It also showed significantly increased IUV-ON/IDark ratio of 2795/2047 (+8/−8 V), 1830/3034 (+3/−3 V), and 502/2094 (+1/−1 V) when compared to that of device A, ascribed to significantly reduced light current due to presence of NCQDs onto the ZnO. The device B upon switching of UV light (1 mW/cm2) (ON/OFF for every 30 s) exhibited the highly stable and reproducible photoresponse curves of current with fast rise/fall times (5.7 s/13.2 s at 1 V) which is much less than the photoresponse (UV-ON) current (with τ r = 10 s/τ d = 14.7 s at 1 V) of device A, indicating lack of photogenerated charge carriers available for UV-ON current due to absorption of UV light by NCQDs in the film b. Upon increasing of bias voltage to 3 V, a small increase in the photoresponse (UV-ON) current is observed with a slight increase in the rise/fall times (7.8 s/12.8 s), ascertaining that the absorption of UV light by NCQDs leads to reduced photoresponse current due to decreased photogenerated carriers. The device B exhibited the linearly increasing Rλ [17/15.7 (+8/−8 V), 4.3/5.1 (+3/−3 V), and 0.6/2.1 mA/W (+1/−1 V)] and EQE (η) [5.7/5.3 (+8/−8 V), 1.5/1.7 (+3/−3 V), and 0.3/0.7% (+1/−1 V)] upon increasing bias voltages (on positive and negative bias) which are found to be significantly less than that of the pristine ZnO based device A, confirming the reduction in the photogenerated charge carries due to presence of NCQDs at the surface and/or grain boundaries of ZnO in the film b. The significantly low-dark current in the device B resulted in slightly increased detectivity (Dλ) [3.8/3.2 (+8/−8 V), 2.7/2.2 (+3/−3 V), and 0.3/1.2 × 1011 Jones (+1/−1 V)] comparing with the detecting ability of device A.

The film c in the UV photodetector (Device C) yields a slightly improved linearly increasing dark current [12.5/11.3 (+8/−8 V), 4.8/3.8 (+3/−3 V), and 1/1.6 nA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] when compared to device B and still significantly less than device A. As reported in the earlier study [24], the slightly increased content of NCQDs in the film c gets adsorbed (chemisorbed) onto the ZnO crystallites in the bulk of the hybrid film and improves the hybrid film quality (improved crystalline properties with reduced defects density) by controlling its size at the nanoscale (by capping), which results in the slightly improved dark current density (Fig. 5). Upon the UV light illumination (365 nm at 1 mW/cm2) onto the film (c) in the device C, it is distinctly observed that the linear light current density [83/96 (+8/−8 V), 33/34 (+3/−3 V), and 9/13 µA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] is highly increased by ∼3 times the light current density of reference device A based on film a as shown in the Fig. 5. The enhanced light current by the improved film quality is not completely ruled out but considered to be significantly less when compared to the level of observed higher light current density. The adsorbed NCQDs (chemisorbed) eventually absorb the ultraviolet light energy and create the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [9,24,28]. The photogenerated electrons in the LUMO (3.9 eV) or near-LUMO sub-band energy levels of NCQDs are transferred to conduction band (4.2 eV) of ZnO in the film c which is consistent to the above PL results corroborated to the energy transfer (non-radiative) [9,24]. The photogenerated electrons transferred from NCQDs to ZnO may significantly contribute to result in the highest light current density and IUV-ON/IDark ratio [6656/8447 (+8/−8 V), 6770/8886 (+3/−3 V), and 8986/7713 (+1/−1 V)] in the device C. The device C exhibited the highly stable and reproducible highest photocurrent on switching of UV light (1 mW/cm2) (ON/OFF for every 30 s) when compared to that of the device B and pristine ZnO based device A (Fig. 6). It showed the considerable increase in the response/recovery times (τ r = 13.2 s/τ d = 17.2 s at 1 V and τ r = 11.8 s/τ d = 17.8 s at −1 V) than that of the reference device A (Table 3), corroborating the transfer of photogenerated electrons from NCQDs to ZnO in the film c. The further increase in the switching photocurrent at the higher bias voltage of ±3 V also confirms the presence of photoelectrons transferred from NCQDs to ZnO to result in such a higher level of photocurrent.

In support of the drastically increased photocurrent in the device C, it revealed a superlinear increase (∼3 times) in the responsivity [96.2 (−8 V), 34 (−3 V), and 12.6 mA/W (−1 V)] and EQE (η) [32.7 (−8 V), 12(−3 V), and 4.3% (−1 V)] when compared to device A, ascertaining that the photogenerated electrons in NCQDs encounter no energy barrier but rather lower conduction band energy level of ZnO and get transferred to contribute to the superlinearly increased device C performances (R and η) (Fig. 7). On reversing the polarity of the bias voltages, it is observed that the device performances of Rλ [83.3 (+8 V), 32.8 (+3 V), and 9 mA/W (+1 V)] and EQE (η) [28.3 (+8 V), 11.1 (+3 V), and 3% (+1 V)] are superlinearly increasing by∼2.7 times higher than that of reference device A, which is slightly less than that of opposite bias and can be attributed to the NCQDs/ZnO interface effect (heterojunction in nanoscale) on ZnO nanoparticles in the film c [9,24,28]. The formation of heterojunction in nanoscale between NCQDs and ZnO nanoparticles in the hybrid thin film is also evident from the existence of chemical interaction of NCQDs [via hydrogen bonding or non-covalent bonding (chemisorption)] and/or charge transfer complex onto the surface of ZnO nanoparticles, which facilitated the energy transfer non-radiatively from NCQDs to ZnO in the NCQDs/ZnO hybrid thin films and resulted in the enhanced PL emission [24]. Such a energy transfer from anchored organic ligand/molecule to ZnO is also consistent with other reports published elsewhere [9,28]. The UV light (365 nm) detecting ability [Dλ: 13.1/15.9 (+8/−8 V), 8.3/9.7 (+3/−3 V), and 5/5.5 × 1011 Jones (+1/−1 V)] of device C is also drastically increased by ∼5.5/7.5 times (in positive/negative bias voltages) than that of the pristine ZnO based device A, showing the contribution of photogenerated electrons transferred to ZnO nanoparticles from NCQDs in the film c for the highest device performance (Fig. 8).

On the increasing content of NCQDs in the UV photodetector (Device D), the UV light (365 nm: 1 mW/cm2) current density is found to be lower [66/58 (+8/−8 V), 25/23 (+3/−3 V), and 8/7.7 µA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] along with decreased IUV-ON/IDark ratio [2874/2557 (+8/−8 V), 2314/2662 (+3/−3 V), and 2037/3189 (+1/−1 V)] when compared to that of the device C, attributed to decreased current conducting/charge transporting routes in the hybrid film due to the covering (capping) of more adsorbed NCQDs onto ZnO nanoparticles. Its slightly increased dark current [23/22.5 (+8/−8 V), 10.9/8.7 (+3/−3 V), and 3.9/2.4 nA/cm2 (+1/−1 V)] can be ascribed to increased film quality due to capping of ZnO nanoparticles (quantum confinement) by incorporated NCQDs. In Fig. 6, the UV light switching response curves at ±1 V show the stable and reproducible response current which is found to be lower than that of device C with the slightly increased rise/decay times (15.4 s/18.4 s at 1 V and 16.9 s/18.3 s at −1 V). Even though the NCQDs observe the UV light and transfer more photogenerated electrons to ZnO nanoparticles, the NCQDs reduce the number of charge transporting routes for the charge collection by respective electrodes in the hybrid film (d) because of the effective capping of ZnO nanoparticles and lead to the increased rise/decay times. Upon increasing the bias voltage to ±3 V, the reversible switching tendency of current is consistent with increased photocurrent and without any significant change in the increased rise/decay times, ascertaining the reduced charge transporting ability of hybrid film (d) in the device D. The device D also exhibited the decreased Rλ [65.8/57.6 (+8/−8 V), 25.3/23.2 (+3/−3 V), and 8/7.7 mA/W (+1/−1 V)] and EQE (η) [22.4/19.6 (+8/−8 V), 8.6/7.9 (+3/−3 V), and 2.7/2.6% (+1/−1 V)], supporting the above results (Table 3). The drastically decreased Dλ [7.7/6.8 (+8/−8 V), 4.3/4.4 (+3/−3 V), and 2.3/2.8 × 1011 Jones (+1/−1 V)] comparing with the device C also confirms the reduced detecting ability of the film d due to decreased photogenerated charge collection efficiency in the device D.

It has been observed that the incorporated NCQDs in the film c (i) get adsorbed onto the surface and/or at the grain boundaries of ZnO crystallites in the bulk of the hybrid thin film and control its size at nanoscale, and (ii) absorb the incident UV light (365 nm) and transfer the absorbed UV energy (non-radiatively) to the conduction band (CB) level and/or the near band energy levels (responsible for the visible emission) of the ZnO nanoparticles. The solution-processed UV (365 nm and 1 mW/cm2) photodetector (device C) exhibited the highest device performances of Rλ and EQE (η) (which are ∼3 times higher), & Dλ (∼7.5 times higher) when compared to that of the reference device A based on pristine film a, ascribed to the fact that under the UV illumination, the NCQDs absorb the UV energy, transfer the photogenerated electrons to ZnO nanoparticles, and enhance the device performances. These results show that the incorporation of NCQDs in the wide-band gap ZnO film significantly improves the device performances of UV photodetector and this hybrid film will be very much useful for various applications of cost-effective solution processed optoelectronic devices and sensors.

The PL decays of spin coated highly transparent NCQDs/ZnO hybrid films show the presence of active surface functional groups for the energy/charge transfer between NCQDs and ZnO. The PL studies ascertain the possible energy transfer to near-band energy levels of ZnO nanoparticles to give rise to its defect related visible emission. The solution processed UV (365 nm and 1 mW/cm²) photodetectors based on hybrid film with NCQDs/ZnO ratio of 1.2 × 10−3 showed the highest responsivity & external quantum efficiency (∼3 times higher), and the detectivity (∼7.5 times higher) than the reference device. The incorporated NCQDs absorb the illuminated UV light and transfer the photogenerated electrons to ZnO nanoparticles, resulting in the higher detector performances. The hybrid films revealing the higher detecting ability (around seven-fold increase when compared to reference device) of UV light (1 mW/cm2) can be the potential candidate for the efficient solution processed UV photodetectors for cost effective applications.
